source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 11.3.3 Using system targets (pp. 206-207)

questions.q: What do systemd targets replace, and what is their purpose?
options: They replace SysV/Upstart run levels; a .target groups other units into a dependency chain
questions.why: Targets (.target units) replace run levels; their job is to group units so a set of services starts together.

questions.q: Show the system's current default target.
questions.why: get-default reports the target that /etc/systemd/system/default.target links to (e.g. graphical.target).

questions.q: Set the default target to multi-user.target.
questions.why: set-default repoints the default.target symlink so the system boots into that target.

questions.q: Switch to the multi-user.target in the CURRENT session (without rebooting).
questions.why: isolate switches the running system to that target immediately.

questions.q: What is the difference between multi-user.target and graphical.target?
options: multi-user is a multi-user system with NO graphical interface; graphical adds the GUI (and also activates multi-user.target)
multi-user has the GUI; graphical is text-only
questions.why: multi-user.target is the text multi-user state; graphical.target starts the GUI on top of it (pulling in multi-user.target).

questions.q: How do rescue mode and emergency mode differ?
options: Rescue mounts local file systems and starts key services (needs root password); emergency is the most minimal - root FS read-only, no other mounts or network
Rescue starts the network; emergency starts the GUI
questions.why: Rescue (the old single-user mode) mounts local FSes and needs the root password; emergency is even more minimal, mounting only root read-only.

questions.q: Switch the current session into rescue mode.
questions.why: systemctl rescue changes the current target to rescue mode; systemctl emergency enters the more minimal emergency mode.
